title: Preparing Data with process_data()
text: process_data() converts raw population counts sampled data frame (one row per icon) ready geom_pop(). handles: Calculating group proportions raw counts Proportionally allocating fixed sample size across groups Supporting hierarchical grouping via high_group_var
code: library(ggpop) library(ggplot2) library(dplyr)

title: Preparing Data with process_data()
text: Required: data, group_var, sum_var. sample_size sets icon count (max 1,000). output contains: type: group label (group_var) n: total count group prop: proportion total One row per sampled icon
code: df_sex <- data.frame(   sex = c("male", "female"),   n   = c(63459580, 67401427) )  df_sex_proc <- process_data(   data        = df_sex,   group_var   = sex,   sum_var     = n,   sample_size = 100 )  head(df_sex_proc) type        n      prop 1   male 63459580 0.4849388 2 female 67401427 0.5150612 3   male 63459580 0.4849388 4 female 67401427 0.5150612 5 female 67401427 0.5150612 6 female 67401427 0.5150612

title: Preparing Data with process_data()
text: sample_size = 100, group 48% population gets ~48 icons.
code: df_sex_proc %>%   group_by(type) %>%   summarise(     icons      = n(),     proportion = round(mean(prop) * 100, 1)   ) # A tibble: 2 × 3   type   icons proportion   <chr>  <int>      <dbl> 1 female    56       51.5 2 male      44       48.5

title: Preparing Data with process_data()
text: Works number groups — just two.
code: df_regions <- data.frame(   region = c("North", "South", "East", "West"),   n      = c(12000, 8000, 15000, 5000) )  df_regions_processed <- process_data(   data        = df_regions,   group_var   = region,   sum_var     = n,   sample_size = 100 )  df_regions_processed %>%   group_by(type) %>%   summarise(icons = n()) # A tibble: 4 × 2   type  icons   <chr> <int> 1 East     44 2 North    28 3 South    15 4 West     13

title: Preparing Data with process_data()
text: Use high_group_var nest groups parent category faceted plots.
code: df_health <- data.frame(   region    = c("North", "South", "East", "West",                 "North", "South", "East", "West"),   status    = c(rep("Healthy", 4), rep("At Risk", 4)),   n         = c(8000, 6000, 9000, 4000,                 4000, 2000, 6000, 1000) )  df_health_processed <- process_data(   data           = df_health,   group_var      = status,   sum_var        = n,   high_group_var = "region",   sample_size    = 100 )  df_health_processed %>%   group_by(group, type) %>%   summarise(icons = n(), .groups = "drop") # A tibble: 8 × 3   group type    icons   <chr> <chr>   <int> 1 East  At Risk    41 2 East  Healthy    59 3 North At Risk    29 4 North Healthy    71 5 South At Risk    35 6 South Healthy    65 7 West  At Risk    24 8 West  Healthy    76

title: Preparing Data with process_data()
text: Optional — pass data directly geom_pop() already one row per icon (max 1,000 rows per plot facet).
code: df_direct <- data.frame(   activity = c(     rep("Walking",  35),     rep("Running",  25),     rep("Hiking",   20),     rep("Cycling",  15),     rep("Swimming",  5)   ),   icon = c(     rep("person-walking",  35),     rep("person-running",  25),     rep("person-hiking",   20),     rep("person-biking",   15),     rep("person-swimming",  5)   ) )  # Pass directly — no process_data() needed ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_direct,     aes(icon = icon, color = activity),     size = 2, dpi = 100, legend_icons = TRUE, arrange = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     "Walking"  = "#5C6BC0",     "Running"  = "#E53935",     "Hiking"   = "#43A047",     "Cycling"  = "#FFB300",     "Swimming" = "#039BE5"   )) +   theme_pop() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 8) +   labs(     title    = "Physical Activity Distribution (n = 100)",     subtitle = "Each icon represents one survey participant",     color    = "Activity"   )

title: Themes & Customization
text: ggpop ships three built-themes, optimized icon-based charts:

title: Themes & Customization
text: Default theme — removes axes gridlines. Layer standard theme() calls top customize.  base_size: controls font size throughout (default: 11) legend_position: "right", "bottom", "left", "top", "none" plot_margin: single number margin() object
code: ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data         = df_sex_proc,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size         = 2,     dpi          = 100,     legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("male" = "#1E88E5", "female" = "#D81B60")) +   theme_pop(base_size = 15) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 5) +   labs(     title    = "Mexico Population by Sex (2024)",     subtitle = "Each icon represents ~1% of the total population",     color    = "Sex"   ) ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data         = df_sex_proc,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size         = 2,     dpi          = 100,     legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("male" = "#1E88E5", "female" = "#D81B60")) +   theme_pop(base_size = 15) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 5) +   labs(     title = "Larger text, legend at bottom",     color = "Sex"   )

title: Themes & Customization
text: base_size: controls font size throughout (default: 11) legend_position: "right", "bottom", "left", "top", "none" plot_margin: single number margin() object
code: ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data         = df_sex_proc,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size         = 2,     dpi          = 100,     legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("male" = "#1E88E5", "female" = "#D81B60")) +   theme_pop(base_size = 15) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 5) +   labs(     title = "Larger text, legend at bottom",     color = "Sex"   )

title: Themes & Customization
text: Dark variant theme_pop() bg_color text_color parameters.  Custom background text colors:
code: ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data         = df_sex_proc,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size         = 2,     dpi          = 100,     legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("male" = "#64B5F6", "female" = "#F48FB1")) +   theme_pop_dark(base_size = 15) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 5) +   labs(     title    = "Mexico Population by Sex (2024)",     subtitle = "Dark theme — ideal for presentations",     color    = "Sex"   ) ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data         = df_sex_proc,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size         = 2,     dpi          = 100,     legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("male" = "#80DEEA", "female" = "#FF8A65")) +   theme_pop_dark(base_size = 15, bg_color = "#0D1B2A", text_color = "#E0F2F1") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 5) +   labs(     title    = "Mexico Population by Sex (2024)",     subtitle = "Custom dark background",     color    = "Sex"   )

title: Themes & Customization
text: title, legend, margins. Best embedding dashboards.
code: ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_sex_proc,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size = 2,     dpi  = 100   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("male" = "#1E88E5", "female" = "#D81B60")) +   theme_pop_minimal(base_size = 15)

title: Themes & Customization
text: Controls legend icon size legend_icons = TRUE.
code: ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data         = df_sex_proc,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size         = 2,     dpi          = 100,     legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("male" = "#1E88E5", "female" = "#D81B60")) +   theme_pop(base_size = 15) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 5) +   labs(title = "Larger legend icons with scale_legend_icon(size = 8)", color = "Sex")

title: Themes & Customization
text: Add outlines icons stroke_width, useful light icons light backgrounds.
code: ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data         = df_sex_proc,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size         = 2,     dpi          = 100,     legend_icons = TRUE,     stroke_width = 8   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("male" = "#1E88E5", "female" = "#D81B60")) +   theme_pop(base_size = 15) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 5) +   labs(title = "Icons with stroke_width = 8", color = "Sex")

title: Themes & Customization
text: Layer theme() calls top theme_pop*() override specific elements.
code: ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data         = df_sex_proc,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size         = 2,     dpi          = 100,     legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("male" = "#1E88E5", "female" = "#D81B60")) +   theme_pop_minimal(base_size=15) +   theme(     plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "#F5F5F5", color = NA),     plot.title       = element_text(color = "#1E88E5", face = "bold", size = 16),     plot.subtitle    = element_text(color = "#546E7A"),     legend.text      = element_text(color = "#37474F"),     legend.position = "bottom",     plot.margin      = margin(20, 20, 20, 20)   ) +   scale_legend_icon(size = 5) +   labs(     title    = "Customized with theme()",     subtitle = "theme_pop() + additional theme() overrides",     color    = "Sex"   )

title: Themes & Customization
text: Visit ggpop website full function reference.

what: Tip 1 — Use valid Font Awesome icon names
title: Tips & Best Practices
text: icon column must contain valid Font Awesome names. Use fa_icons() search. find valid icon names
code: # Search by keyword fa_icons(query = "person") fa_icons(query = "car") fa_icons(query = "heart") df_tip1 <- data.frame(   transport = rep(c("Car", "Bicycle", "Plane"), each = 10),   icon      = rep(c("car", "bicycle", "plane"), each = 10),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_tip1,     aes(icon = icon, color = transport),     size = 3, dpi = 100, legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     "Car"     = "#E64A19",     "Bicycle" = "#2E7D32",     "Plane"   = "#1565C0"   )) +   theme_pop() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 8) +   labs(title = "Tip 1: Valid FA icon names", color = "Transport")

title: Tips & Best Practices
text: ggpop uses several internal column names layout computation. data already contains names, geom throw error: Rename conflicting columns calling geom_pop() geom_icon_point().
code: # Safe column names — no conflict with internal reserved names df_tip2 <- data.frame(   sex      = rep(c("Male", "Female"), each = 20),   icon     = rep(c("mars", "venus"), each = 20),   age_grp  = rep(c("Adult", "Child"), times = 20),  # safe name   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_tip2,     aes(icon = icon, color = sex),     size = 3, dpi = 100, legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("Male" = "#1565C0", "Female" = "#C62828")) +   theme_pop() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 8) +   labs(title = "Tip 2: Safe column names", color = "Sex")

title: Tips & Best Practices
text: geom_pop() renders icons raster images. means: fill supported → hard error color correct aesthetic icon color alpha supported — either fixed parameter (geom_pop(alpha = 0.5)) mapped via aes(alpha = col), alpha reserved column name — rename column first (e.g. opacity)
code: # fill will error in geom_pop(): aes(icon = icon, group = sex, fill = sex)   # Error df_tip3 <- data.frame(   region = rep(c("North", "South", "East", "West"), each = 15),   icon   = rep(c("compass", "arrow-down", "arrow-right", "arrow-left"), each = 15),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_tip3,     aes(icon = icon, color = region,  alpha = 0.5),                       # fixed alpha ✓ — works as a      size = 3, dpi = 100, legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     "North" = "#00897B", "South" = "#E64A19",     "East"  = "#7B1FA2", "West"  = "#F9A825"   )) +   theme_pop() +   scale_legend_icon(size = 8) +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(title = "Tip 3: Use color, not fill", color = "Region")

what: Tip 4 — Keep your total icon count at or below 1,000
title: Tips & Best Practices
text: Max 1,000 icons per facet panel — exceeding raises error. Use process_data(sample_size = ...) stay within limits.
code: df_tip4_raw <- data.frame(   group = c("Group A", "Group B", "Group C"),   n     = c(4500000, 3200000, 2100000) )  # process_data samples down to sample_size icons total df_tip4 <- process_data(   data        = df_tip4_raw,   group_var   = group,   sum_var     = n,   sample_size = 100          # stays well under the 1,000 limit ) %>%   mutate(icon = case_when(     type == "Group A" ~ "circle",     type == "Group B" ~ "square",     type == "Group C" ~ "triangle-exclamation"   ))  ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_tip4,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size = 2, dpi = 100, legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     "Group A" = "#1565C0",     "Group B" = "#2E7D32",     "Group C" = "#E64A19"   )) +   theme_pop() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 8) +   labs(     title    = "Tip 4: Use process_data() to control icon count",     subtitle = "sample_size = 100 — well under the 1,000-icon limit",     color    = "Group"   )

what: Tip 5 — Don’t map x or y in geom_pop()
title: Tips & Best Practices
text: Don’t map x y aes() — ggpop computes positions internally warn . warn (x/y silently ignored)
code: geom_pop(data = df, aes(icon = icon, group = sex, x = sex, y = sex)) df_tip5 <- data.frame(   type = rep(c("Urban", "Rural"), each = 25),   icon = rep(c("building", "tree"), each = 25),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Correct: no x or y in aes() — geom_pop() handles layout automatically ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_tip5,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size = 3, dpi = 100, legend_icons = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("Urban" = "#5C6BC0", "Rural" = "#388E3C")) +   theme_pop() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 8) +   labs(title = "Tip 5: No x/y mapping needed in geom_pop()", color = "Area")

what: Tip 6 — One icon per legend group when using legend_icons = TRUE
title: Tips & Best Practices
text: color group must map exactly one icon name legend_icons = TRUE.
code: # Each group maps to exactly one icon — clean legend df_tip6 <- data.frame(   species = rep(c("Birds", "Fish", "Trees"), each = 20),   icon    = rep(c("dove", "fish", "tree"), each = 20),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_tip6,     aes(icon = icon, color = species),     size = 3, dpi = 100,     legend_icons = TRUE            # one icon per group ✓ — no warning   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     "Birds" = "#0277BD",     "Fish"  = "#00897B",     "Trees" = "#2E7D32"   )) +   theme_pop() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 8) +   labs(title = "Tip 6: One icon per legend group", color = "Species")

what: Tip 7 — scale_legend_icon() must come after all theme calls
title: Tips & Best Practices
text: theme() theme_*() calls must come scale_legend_icon() — theme call resets key size. Wrong order — theme() scale_legend_icon() resets key size
code: ggplot(...) +   geom_pop(...) +   scale_legend_icon(size = 10) +   # ← scale_legend_icon first   theme_pop()                       # ← then theme — WRONG df_tip7 <- data.frame(   status = rep(c("Active", "Inactive"), each = 25),   icon   = rep(c("circle-check", "circle-xmark"), each = 25),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Correct order: theme calls BEFORE scale_legend_icon() ggplot(data = df_tip7,     aes(icon = icon, color = status)) +   geom_pop(size = 3, dpi = 100, legend_icons = TRUE) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("Active" = "#2E7D32", "Inactive" = "#C62828")) +   theme_pop() +                     # theme first   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 10) +    # scale_legend_icon last   labs(     title    = "Tip 7: Correct layer order",     subtitle = "theme calls → scale_legend_icon()",     color    = "Status"   )

what: Tip 8 — Use process_data() to convert count data
title: Tips & Best Practices
text: data one row per group count column, use process_data() expand one row per icon.
code: # Your raw count data df_counts_raw <- data.frame(   education = c("No degree", "High school", "Bachelor's", "Graduate"),   population = c(12500000, 38000000, 45000000, 18000000) )  # Expand to one row per icon df_edu <- process_data(   data        = df_counts_raw,   group_var   = education,   sum_var     = population,   sample_size = 100 ) %>%   mutate(icon = case_when(     type == "No degree"   ~ "xmark",     type == "High school" ~ "school",     type == "Bachelor's"  ~ "graduation-cap",     type == "Graduate"    ~ "user-graduate"   ))  df_edu$type <- factor(df_edu$type,   levels = c("No degree", "High school", "Bachelor's", "Graduate"))  ggplot(data = df_edu, aes(icon = icon, color = type)) +   geom_pop(size = 2, dpi = 100, legend_icons = TRUE, arrange = TRUE) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     "No degree"   = "#B71C1C",     "High school" = "#E65100",     "Bachelor's"  = "#1565C0",     "Graduate"    = "#1B5E20"   )) +   theme_pop() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 6) +   labs(     title    = "Tip 8: Use process_data() for count-based data",     subtitle = "Each icon ≈ 1% of the total population sample",     color    = "Education"   )

what: Tip 9 — Use seed for reproducible charts
title: Tips & Best Practices
text: Pass seed geom_pop() reproducible layout — useful papers, dashboards, automated pipelines. matters including chart paper, dashboard, automated report, want output identical every time code runs. fixed seed guarantees .
code: df_tip9_raw <- data.frame(   source     = c("Coal", "Natural Gas", "Nuclear", "Renewables"),   generation = c(800000, 1600000, 700000, 900000) )  df_tip9 <- process_data(   data        = df_tip9_raw,   group_var   = source,   sum_var     = generation,   sample_size = 100 ) %>%   mutate(icon = case_when(     type == "Coal"        ~ "industry",     type == "Natural Gas" ~ "fire",     type == "Nuclear"     ~ "atom",     type == "Renewables"  ~ "sun"   ))  ggplot(data = df_tip9, aes(icon = icon, color = type)) +   geom_pop(size = 2, dpi = 100, seed = 42) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     "Coal"        = "#B0BEC5",     "Natural Gas" = "#FFB74D",     "Nuclear"     = "#CE93D8",     "Renewables"  = "#4DB6AC"   )) +   theme_pop() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 8) +   labs(     title    = "U.S. Electricity Generation by Source",     subtitle = "Each icon represents ~1% of total generation — layout fixed with seed = 42",     color    = "Energy source"   )

what: Tip 10 — Use arrange = TRUE to cluster icons by group
title: Tips & Best Practices
text: Use arrange = FALSE (default) scattered effect. Use arrange = TRUE cluster icons group, making boundaries groups visible.
code: df_tip12 <- data.frame(   outcome = c(rep("Recovered", 65), rep("Ongoing", 25), rep("Worsened", 10)),   icon    = c(rep("circle-check", 65), rep("arrow-right", 25), rep("circle-xmark", 10)),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  df_tip12$outcome <- factor(df_tip12$outcome,   levels = c("Recovered", "Ongoing", "Worsened"))  ggplot(data = df_tip12, aes(icon = icon, color = outcome)) +   geom_pop(size = 2, dpi = 100, legend_icons = TRUE, arrange = TRUE) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     "Recovered" = "#2E7D32",     "Ongoing"   = "#F9A825",     "Worsened"  = "#C62828"   )) +   theme_pop() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 8) +   labs(     title    = "Tip 12: arrange = TRUE clusters icons by group",     subtitle = "Groups appear contiguously — proportional boundaries are clear",     color    = "Outcome"   )

what: Tip 11 — Per-group alpha: rename the column and hide the alpha legend entry
title: Tips & Best Practices
text: want different transparency per group, follow three-step workflow: Rename transparency column — alpha reserved internal name, use opacity name Map aes(): aes(alpha = opacity) Suppress auto-generated alpha legend guides(alpha = "none") — color legend already encodes information Skipping step 3 leaves unwanted legend entry. Skipping step 1 causes error.
code: # Error — alpha is a reserved internal column name in ggpop df$alpha <- c(1.0, 0.6, 0.3) aes(alpha = alpha)   # Error df_tip13 <- data.frame(   status  = c(rep("Confirmed", 60), rep("Probable", 25), rep("Suspected", 15)),   icon    = c(rep("circle-check", 60), rep("clock", 25), rep("circle-question", 15)),   opacity = c(rep(1.0, 60), rep(0.6, 25), rep(0.3, 15))  # ← named "opacity", not "alpha" )  df_tip13$status <- factor(df_tip13$status,   levels = c("Confirmed", "Probable", "Suspected"))  ggplot(data = df_tip13, aes(icon = icon, color = status, alpha = opacity)) +   geom_pop(size = 2, dpi = 100, legend_icons = TRUE) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     "Confirmed" = "#1565C0",     "Probable"  = "#6A1B9A",     "Suspected" = "#E64A19"   )) +   guides(alpha = "none") + #step 3: hide the auto alpha legend   theme_pop() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 8) +   labs(     title    = "Tip 13: Per-group alpha — rename column, map it, hide the extra legend",     subtitle = "opacity → aes(alpha = opacity) + guides(alpha = 'none')",     color    = "Case status"   )

what: Tip 12 — With facet_wrap(), the icon limit applies per panel
title: Tips & Best Practices
text: geom_pop() enforces maximum 1,000 icons per facet panel — globally. Four panels 250 icons (1,000 total) well within limits; two panels 1,001 error. Use high_group_var process_data() sample independently per panel keep sample_size low enough single panel exceeds cap. Tell geom_pop() column drives faceting passing facet = group. Rule thumb Keep sample_size ≤ 1,000 ÷ number panels leave headroom every panel.
code: df_tip14_raw <- data.frame(   region = rep(c("North", "South", "East", "West"), each = 2),   sex    = rep(c("Male", "Female"), times = 4),   n      = c(2100000, 1900000, 3200000, 2800000, 1500000, 1400000, 2700000, 2500000) )  # 50 icons per panel × 4 panels = 200 total — well within the 1,000-per-panel limit df_tip14 <- process_data(   data           = df_tip14_raw,   high_group_var = "region",   group_var      = sex,   sum_var        = n,   sample_size    = 50 ) %>%   mutate(icon = case_when(     type == "Male"   ~ "mars",     type == "Female" ~ "venus"   ))  ggplot(data = df_tip14, aes(icon = icon, color = type)) +   geom_pop(size = 2, dpi = 100, legend_icons = TRUE, facet = group) +   facet_wrap(~ group) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("Male" = "#1565C0", "Female" = "#C62828")) +   theme_pop() +   theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         legend.title = element_text(color = "white"),         strip.text= element_text(color = "white"),         legend.position = "bottom") +   scale_legend_icon(size = 8) +   labs(     title    = "Tip 14: Facet icon limit is per panel",     subtitle = "50 icons per panel × 4 panels — each well within the 1,000-per-panel cap",     color    = "Sex"   )

what: Tip 13 – Combine an icon legend with a separate fill legend
title: Tips & Best Practices
text: want icon legend (via geom_icon_point) unrelated fill legend (e.g. dominance status) plot, use two dummy data layers – one per legend – inherit.aes = FALSE. keeps aesthetics completely isolated prevents icons bleeding fill legend keys. Key rules dummy_icons: contains icon group columns. Drives icon legend. dummy_status: contains status column – icon column. Drives fill legend. use inherit.aes = FALSE inherit top-level aes(). main geom_icon_point() layer uses show.legend = FALSE – legend driven entirely dummy layers.
code: status_cols <- c("ND" = "#06D6A0", "ED" = "#1A78C2", "D" = "#E69F00")  df_cea <- data.frame(   effect      = c(277.25, 277.57, 277.78, 277.83, 277.76) / 12,   cost        = c(26000, 27000, 28020, 28440, 29440),   strategy    = c("Status Quo", "One-Time", "Every 5yr", "Every 3yr", "Annual"),   group_label = c("Status Quo", "Infrequent", "Infrequent", "Frequent", "Frequent"),   icon_col    = c("person", "vial", "vial", "syringe", "syringe"),   status      = factor(c("ND", "ND", "ND", "ND", "D"), levels = c("ND", "ED", "D")),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) dummy_icons <- data.frame(   effect      = rep(NA_real_, 3), cost = rep(NA_real_, 3),   icon_col    = c("person", "vial", "syringe"),   group_label = factor(c("Status Quo", "Infrequent", "Frequent"),                        levels = c("Status Quo", "Infrequent", "Frequent")),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) dummy_status <- data.frame(   effect = rep(NA_real_, 2), cost = rep(NA_real_, 2),   status = factor(c("ND", "D"), levels = c("ND", "ED", "D")),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) dummy_ef <- data.frame(effect = NA_real_, cost = NA_real_,                        frontier = "Efficient Frontier")  suppressWarnings(   ggplot(df_cea, aes(x = effect, y = cost, icon = icon_col, color = status)) +     geom_line(data = df_cea %>% filter(status == "ND") %>% arrange(effect),               aes(x = effect, y = cost, group = 1),               color = "#06D6A0", linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.6, inherit.aes = FALSE) +     geom_icon_point(size = 2, dpi = 120, show.legend = FALSE) +     geom_icon_point(data = dummy_icons,                     aes(x = effect, y = cost, icon = icon_col, color = group_label),                     size = 2, dpi = 120, inherit.aes = FALSE, show.legend = TRUE) +     geom_point(data = dummy_status, aes(x = effect, y = cost, fill = status),                shape = 22, size = 0, alpha = 0, inherit.aes = FALSE, show.legend = TRUE) +     geom_point(data = dummy_ef, aes(x = effect, y = cost, fill = frontier),                shape = NA, size = 0, alpha = 0, inherit.aes = FALSE,                show.legend = TRUE, key_glyph = "path") +     geom_label(data = df_cea %>% filter(status != "ND"),                aes(x = effect, y = cost, label = strategy, fill = status),                color = "white", size = 2.5, vjust = -1.5,                label.size = NA, fontface = "bold",                inherit.aes = FALSE, show.legend = FALSE) +     geom_label(data = df_cea %>% filter(status == "ND"),                aes(x = effect, y = cost, label = strategy),                color = "white", fill = "#06D6A0", size = 2.5,                hjust = -0.1, label.size = NA, fontface = "bold",                inherit.aes = FALSE, show.legend = FALSE) +     scale_color_manual(       name   = "HIV Screening",       values = c(status_cols, "Status Quo" = "#2C3E50",                  "Infrequent" = "#2C3E50", "Frequent" = "#2C3E50"),       breaks = c("Status Quo", "Infrequent", "Frequent"),       guide  = guide_legend(order = 1, ncol = 3,                             override.aes = list(alpha = 1, size = 4,                                                 color = "#2C3E50", fill = NA, shape = NA))     ) + title: fa_icons()
text: frequently used categories example names: Use fa_icons(query = "...") explore category. name column result goes icon column icon parameter.

title: geom_icon_point()
text: Use icon parameter (aes()) display one icon across points.
code: ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)) +   geom_icon_point(icon = "seedling", size = 1, dpi = 72) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     setosa     = "#43A047",     versicolor = "#1E88E5",     virginica  = "#E53935"   )) +   labs(title = "Fixed icon", x = "Sepal Length", y = "Petal Length")

title: geom_icon_point()
text: Map icon inside aes() assign different icon group.
code: library(ggplot2) library(ggpop)  df_food <- data.frame(   food     = c("Apple", "Carrot", "Orange", "Chicken", "Beef", "Salmon",                "Milk", "Cheese", "Yogurt"),   calories = c(52, 41, 47, 165, 250, 208, 61, 402, 59),   protein  = c(0.3, 1.1, 0.9, 31, 26, 20, 3.2, 25, 10),   group    = c(rep("Fruit", 3), rep("Meat", 3), rep("Dairy", 3)),   icon     = c("apple-whole", "carrot", "lemon",                "drumstick-bite", "bacon", "fish",                "bottle-water", "cheese", "jar") )  ggplot(df_food, aes(x = calories, y = protein, icon = icon, color = food)) +   geom_icon_point(size = 2, dpi = 100) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     "Apple" = "#FF5252", "Carrot" = "#FFA726", "Orange" = "#FFB74D",     "Chicken" = "#8D6E63", "Beef" = "#6D4C41",     "Salmon" = "#EF5350", "Milk" = "#42A5F5", "Cheese" = "#FFD54F",      "Yogurt" = "#4DB6AC"   )) +   labs(     title = "Calories vs. Protein by Food Group",     subtitle = "Each icon represents a specific food; color reflects the group",     x = "Calories (per 100g)",     y = "Protein (g per 100g)",     color = "Food Group"   )

title: geom_pop()
text: Verify icon names fa_icons(query = "biking") fa_icons(query = "swimming") Font Awesome naming can vary slightly version.
code: df_raw <- data.frame(   activity = c("Running", "Cycling", "Swimming", "Walking"),   n        = c(28, 22, 18, 32) )  df_plot <- process_data(   data        = df_raw,   group_var   = activity,   sum_var     = n,   sample_size = 30 ) %>%   mutate(icon = case_when(     type == "Running"  ~ "person-running",     type == "Cycling"  ~ "person-biking",     type == "Swimming" ~ "person-swimming",     type == "Walking"  ~ "person-walking"   ))  ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_plot,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size = 3, dpi = 72   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     Running  = "#e76f51",     Cycling  = "#2a9d8f",     Swimming = "#457b9d",     Walking  = "#e9c46a"   )) +   theme_pop() +   scale_legend_icon(size = 7) +   labs(title = "Preferred physical activity", color = NULL)

title: geom_pop()
text: Use high_group_var facet panel variable group_var color/icon variable within panel. Icons different groups scatter mix across panel default (arrange = FALSE).
code: df_raw <- data.frame(   region    = c("North", "North", "North",                 "South", "South", "South",                 "East",  "East",  "East",                 "West",  "West",  "West"),   work_type = c("Office", "Remote", "Hybrid",                 "Office", "Remote", "Freelance",                 "Remote", "Hybrid", "Freelance",                 "Office", "Hybrid", "Freelance"),   n         = c(40, 35, 25,                 20, 45, 35,                 50, 30, 20,                 30, 40, 30) )  df_plot <- process_data(   data           = df_raw,   group_var      = work_type,   sum_var        = n,   sample_size    = 10,   high_group_var = "region" ) %>%   mutate(icon = case_when(     type == "Office"    ~ "building",     type == "Remote"    ~ "house",     type == "Hybrid"    ~ "shuffle",     type == "Freelance" ~ "mug-hot"   ))  ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_plot,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size = 2, dpi = 72, facet = group   ) +   facet_wrap(~ group, ncol = 2) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     Office    = "#2a9d8f",     Remote    = "#e76f51",     Hybrid    = "#457b9d",     Freelance = "#e9c46a"   )) +   theme_pop() +   labs(title = "Work type by region", color = NULL)

title: geom_pop()
text: arrange = TRUE groups icons category instead scattering .
code: df_browser <- data.frame(   browser = c("Chrome", "Firefox", "Safari"),   n       = c(45, 20, 25) )  df_browser_plot <- process_data(   data        = df_browser,   group_var   = browser,   sum_var     = n,   sample_size = 20 ) %>%   mutate(icon = case_when(     type == "Chrome"  ~ "chrome",     type == "Firefox" ~ "firefox",     type == "Safari"  ~ "safari",     type == "Edge"    ~ "edge"   ))  ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data    = df_browser_plot,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size    = 2, dpi = 72, arrange = TRUE   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(     Chrome  = "#4285F4",     Firefox = "#FF7139",     Safari  = "#006CFF",     Edge    = "#0078D7"   )) +   theme_pop() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") +   labs(title = "Browser market share", color = NULL)

title: Getting Started with ggpop
code: df_raw <- data.frame(   sex = c("Female", "Male"),   n   = c(55, 45) )  df_plot <- process_data(   data        = df_raw,   group_var   = sex,   sum_var     = n,   sample_size = 40 ) %>%   mutate(icon = case_when(     type == "Female" ~ "person-dress",     type == "Male"   ~ "person"   ))  ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_plot,     aes(icon = icon, color = type),     size = 2.5, dpi = 72   ) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(Female = "#C0392B", Male = "#2980B9")) +   theme_pop() +   scale_legend_icon(size = 5) +   labs(title = "Population by sex", color = NULL)

title: process_data()
text: Use high_group_var data faceting variable. samples independently within panel proportions correct per group. group column output contains panel label — pass facet_wrap(~group) geom_pop(facet = group).
code: df_region_raw <- data.frame(   region = c("North", "North", "South", "South"),   sex    = c("Female", "Male", "Female", "Male"),   n      = c(30, 20, 25, 25) )  df_region <- process_data(   data           = df_region_raw,   group_var      = sex,   sum_var        = n,   sample_size    = 20,   high_group_var = "region" )  head(df_region, 4) #>   group   type  n prop #> 1 North Female 30  0.6 #> 2 North Female 30  0.6 #> 3 North Female 30  0.6 #> 4 North Female 30  0.6

Turn data stories. ggpop R package built top ggplot2 simplifies creation icon-based population charts. combining features ggplot2 ggimage, ggpop lets users visualize population data using customizable icons arranged circular layouts. Designed primarily visual storytelling, ggpop helps users communicate population statistics appealing manner.

title: Icon-Based Population Charts and Plots for ggplot2
text: dataset df_pop_mx minimal example illustrating population counts sex Mexico 2024. sex: categorical variable indicating sex ("male" / "female") n: numeric variable representing population size sex category country: constant value "Mexico" continent: constant value "America"
code: library(dplyr) library(ggpop)  df_pop_mx <- data.frame(sex = c("male", "female"),                         n = c(63459580, 67401427),                         country = "Mexico",                         continent = "America")

title: Icon-Based Population Charts and Plots for ggplot2
text: apply process_data() function population data df_pop_mx following parameters: group_var = sex: groups data sex (male/female). grouping variable sum_var = n: uses column n (population counts) group totals. variable summed calculate proportions. sample_size = 1000: generates 1,000 sampled records, proportionally allocated group. package allows sample size 1000. function calculates group proportions, performs sampling create new data frame (df_pop_mx_prop). row represents one draw 1,000 samples. Notable columns: type: group (male female) sampled. n: total population count corresponding group. prop: proportion group overall dataset. Note: process_data() optional. can pass data frame directly geom_pop() — long row represents one icon. maximum 1,000 rows per plot (can pass per facet group).
code: df_pop_mx_prop <- process_data(data = df_pop_mx,                                 group_var = sex,                                 sum_var = n,                                 sample_size = 1000)

what: Search and list Font Awesome icons — fa_icons
title: Search and list Font Awesome icons — fa_icons
text: Retrieves Font Awesome icon names, optionally filtered search query category. Results can returned plain character vector tibble category classification.

title: Search and list Font Awesome icons — fa_icons
code: fa_icons(   query = NULL,   category = NULL,   regex = FALSE,   classify = TRUE,   include_unclassified = TRUE,   class_map = NULL,   primary_only = TRUE,   as_vector = FALSE )

title: Search and list Font Awesome icons — fa_icons
text: query Character string. Filter icons whose names contain query. Set NULL (default) return icons. regex = TRUE, query treated Perl-compatible regular expression. category Character vector. One category names filter . Run fa_categories() see valid options. Setting category implies classify = TRUE. regex Logical. TRUE, query interpreted Perl-compatible regular expression. Default FALSE (fixed-string match). classify Logical. TRUE (default), icon classified categories using class_map primary_class column included returned tibble. Ignored as_vector = TRUE category = NULL. include_unclassified Logical. FALSE, icons match category pattern dropped. Default TRUE. class_map named list mapping category names regex patterns. Defaults internal .fa_default_class_map(). primary_only Logical. TRUE (default), tibble contains primary_class column omits all_classes. as_vector Logical. TRUE, return plain sorted character vector icon names instead tibble. category = NULL, classification skipped entirely. Default FALSE.

title: Search and list Font Awesome icons — fa_icons
text: as_vector = TRUE, sorted character vector icon names. Otherwise tibble columns: icon Icon name (character). primary_class Primary category icon belongs , NA unclassified (character). all_classes matching categories (list-column character vectors). present primary_only = FALSE.

Why you are seeing this warning: #>   The legend can only display ONE icon per group, but some groups have #>   multiple: #>    #> - A: 2 icons (circle, heart) #> - B: 3 icons (star, heart, circle) #> - C: 2 icons (circle, heart) #>    #> ℹ What happens: #>   - The most frequent icon for each group will be shown in the legend #>   - Other icons in that group will still appear in the plot #>   - This may confuse viewers if icons have different meanings #>    #> ℹ Recommended fixes: #>    #>   Option 1: Use consistent icons per group #>   `df <- df %>% mutate(icon = case_when(` #>   `sex == 'A' ~ 'male',` #>   `sex == 'B' ~ 'female'` #>   `))` #>    #>   Option 2: Create a separate grouping variable #>   `df <- df %>% mutate(group = paste(sex, icon, sep = '_'))` #>   `ggplot() + geom_pop(aes(icon = icon, color = group))` #>    #>   Option 3: Set legend_icons = FALSE to use point markers #>   `geom_pop(..., legend_icons = FALSE)`   # Use a fixed icon ggplot(data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = category)) +   geom_icon_point(icon = "star")  # }

title: ggpop 1.7.0
text: release ggpop delivers new theming support, expanded icon customization, critical bug fixes, significant internal refactoring toward fully ggplot-native architecture. Deprecated functionality removed package finalized.

title: ggpop 1.7.0
text: Fixed stroke_width effect geom_icon_point() placed inside aes(). validator validate_stroke_width_not_aesthetic() already used geom_pop() missing geom_icon_point(), causing parameter silently bypassed. geoms now warn users move stroke_width outside aes() (#353). Fixed literal expression alpha values aes() (e.g. aes(alpha = 0.5) aes(alpha = col / 10)) applying legend icons. column-mapped alpha previously affected legend; fixed constants computed expressions now correctly resolved applied plot icons legend keys geom_pop() geom_icon_point() (#353). Fixed false “Facet / grouping caution” warning incorrect per-group icon positioning triggered user-provided data frame happened contain column named group. warning auto-facet detection now gated whether data produced process_data(), raw data frames work correctly regardless column names (#346). Fixed icon size inconsistency across different ggplot2 themes corrected scale_legend_icon() properly reflect theme settings (#287). Fixed legend icon ordering factor variables mapped colour, ensuring legend icons follow factor level order rather data row order (#294). Fixed icon bleed unrelated legends geom_icon_point(). Icons rendered behind fill legend keys geom_image lists fill default aesthetics, causing ggplot2 invoke key_glyph_icon_point fill guide keys. Fixed setting show.legend = c(colour = ..., fill = FALSE) exclude layer fill guides, fallback guard key_glyph_icon_point returning blank grob icon label matches (#371). Fixed icon-label mismatch geom_icon_point() using dummy data frame inherit.aes = FALSE. Inherited plot-level mappings overriding layer’s mappings combined_mapping, causing icons resolve alphabetically icon name instead group label. Fixed giving layer mappings priority inherited ones (#371).

title: ggpop 1.7.0
text: Refactored internal geom functions fully ggplot-native, using plot-scoped layer computation removing reliance last_plot() layer construction (#266). Added validate_alpha_column() check values column mapped alpha construction time. Values > 1 <= 0 abort descriptive message rescaling hint; values (0, 0.1) trigger low-alpha warning (#353). Added validate_literal_alpha_in_aes() validate literal expression alpha values inside aes(), applying range rules fixed alpha parameter (#353). Added robustness unit tests stroke_width parameter validate behavior across edge cases (#269). Expanded unit tests internal helper functions incorporated code improvements reliability maintainability (#275). Cleaned error warning messages removing file-level path details, replacing concise, user-facing descriptions (#277). Normalized icon size calculation ensure consistent rendering geom_pop geom_icon_point (#282). Added unit tests visual snapshot tests scale_legend_icon validate legend sizing rendering behavior (#289).

title: ggpop 1.7.0
text: Added stroke_width parameter geom_icon_point, allowing users control icon outline thickness improved visual contrast (#268). Introduced personalized ggpop themes (e.g., theme_pop) consistent, opinionated plot styling box (#291). Added show.legend support geom_icon_point() geom_pop(). Passing show.legend = FALSE now correctly suppresses layer’s legend entries, matching standard ggplot2 behaviour. fix sets argument directly layer object construction, bypassing ggimage::geom_image() honour parameter (#337). Added fa_icons() allow users search list available Font Awesome icons name, category, regex pattern. Results can returned classified tibble plain character vector. Icon names cached session-wide avoid repeated metadata calls (#340). geom_pop() now exposes internally computed coordinates downstream layers. Geoms geom_text() geom_label() can added geom_pop() without specifying x y explicitly — inherited automatically icon grid (#341).

title: ggpop 1.7.0
text: Removed deprecated caption_pop function. Users migrate standard ggplot2 annotation approaches (#279). Finalized public function names key glyph implementations geom_pop geom_icon_point, standardizing package. Existing code relying previous internal names may require updates (#281).

title: ggpop 1.6.1
text: Fixed icon column mapping geom_pop geom_icon_point, ensuring users can map icons custom column name without rendering errors unexpected behavior (#255). Fixed aspect ratio preservation legend icon rendering, ensuring icons scale intelligently within legend box maintaining proportions (#254).

title: ggpop 1.6.1
text: Refactored process_data function comprehensive input validation, enhanced error handling, improved calculation proportions, including proper handling NA values filtering invalid data (#243). Enhanced legend icon sizing logic automatically scale icons within legend box preserving aspect ratio, wide icons filling horizontally tall icons filling vertically (#253). Improved error warning message formatting throughout package better readability consistency, including multi-line formatting actionable guidance (#253). Added comprehensive unit tests process_data function covering various scenarios including hierarchical grouping, input validation, edge cases, reproducibility (#242). Improved code clarity structure across multiple functions refactoring argument formatting, removing unnecessary whitespace, adding thematic sections comments (#253). Enhanced cross-layer validation moving legend icon consistency checks plot-add hook, ensuring layers considered detecting mixed legend_icons settings (#258).

title: ggpop 1.6.0
